title: Contracts · Bridge
scan: What you will learn: The four contract types — API, Event, Data, Component — and how each defines a system boundary that AI agents must not cross
Why it matters: Without contracts every AI-generated PR must be reviewed from first principles — with contracts review scope collapses to "does this satisfy the contract?"
API contracts (OpenAPI/GraphQL schema) are the most mature; Event contracts (type registry + payload schema) are the most frequently skipped and the most damaging when absent
Contracts are the safety net for AI-generated code — violations are caught at CI schema validation before merge, not in production
A contract that is not version-controlled does not exist — it is a verbal agreement that degrades over time

sections.title: Contracts — what architects actually talk about

blocks.html: mplementation can change tomorrow. A contract is the promise that must hold. Once you start thinking in contracts you stop arguing about how code is written and start arguing about what it promises to do — which is a much shorter and more useful conversation.

blocks.title: Contract pipeline
blocks.source:
flowchart TD
    A[Architect defines contract] --> B[contracts repo\nversioned]
    B --> C[AI generates implementation]
    C --> D[Engineer reviews against contract]
    D --> E[CI validates schema]
    E -->|pass| F[merge approved]
    E -->|fail| C

blocks.html: Contracts are the prerequisite for agentic development. An AI agent without a contract boundary optimises for local coherence — the generated code works in isolation — at the expense of system coherence. The agent doesn't know it is breaking a downstream consumer. The contract is the architect's instruction to the agent about where the system boundary is.

Treating the API response shape as the API contract. The contract includes error codes, rate limits, authentication method, pagination contract, versioning policy, and SLA — not just the happy-path JSON response. A consumer built against only the response shape will break on the first non-200 status it was not designed for.

sections.title: The four contracts in any system

blocks.html: The four contracts in any system

cards.title: API Contract
cards.body: Endpoints, methods, payload shapes, error codes, rate limits, versioning. The promise to the world outside your codebase.

cards.title: Event Contract
cards.body: The event name (domain:action), its payload shape, who produces it, who may consume it. The promise between modules.

cards.title: Data Contract
cards.body: The schema of an entity — mandatory and optional fields, validation rules, allowed transitions. The promise about the data itself.

cards.title: Component Contract
cards.body: Props in, events out, slots/children. The public surface of a reusable building block.

sections.title: Why contracts are the bridge to AI-generated code

blocks.html: Why contracts are the bridge to AI-generated code

This is the most important paragraph of Part I. Contracts are what you hand to AI; implementations are what AI hands back. The architect owns the contracts; the AI fills in the implementations. Get the contracts right and the implementation is mostly mechanical. Get them wrong and no amount of clever prompting will fix it.
This is also why the Stalwart prompt (inside CODER's C) begins by stating the file contract, the event contract, and the data flow — before naming a single feature. The architect's leverage is here.
An architect who controls the contracts controls the architecture. An architect who only reviews code reviews symptoms.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Contracts
items: Is the contract written down, not just in someone's head?
Who enforces it — types, tests, manual review?
Who must be notified when it changes?
Does the AI prompt template reference it?
